skill.name: remotion-best-practices
skill.description: Guidance for building Remotion videos with React components, media assets, captions, animations, and rendering best practices.
skill.summary: Use Remotion patterns for React video composition, timing, assets, captions, and animation.
skill.author: remotion-dev

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many high-risk patterns, but review found they are mainly Markdown code fences, frontmatter text, Remotion examples, and documentation links. No evidence found of prompt injection, hidden scripts, credential access, or malicious exfiltration. The remaining concerns are low-risk examples that may lead generated projects to install packages or fetch remote media.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity blocker findings point to skill names, descriptions, headings, and Markdown content. I did not find cryptographic code, hashing, encryption, or security-sensitive algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are frontmatter descriptions or links to rule files. They contain no cryptographic API calls or executable security logic.

low_findings.title: Fenced Install Commands Misclassified as Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are Remotion package installation examples inside Markdown code fences. They are not executed by the skill, but users should review commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced bash snippets for installing Remotion packages. There is no wrapper script or skill code that executes them automatically.

low_findings.title: Remote Fetch Examples Require Input Review
low_findings.description: Several examples show fetching remote media, Lottie data, subtitles, or metadata. This is normal Remotion guidance, but generated applications should validate untrusted URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch calls are visible documentation examples, not concealed network behavior. Risk remains if a user copies them with untrusted prop-controlled URLs.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Finding Is a Relative Import Example
low_findings.description: The filesystem finding points to a relative import in sample TypeScript. It does not read arbitrary files or construct paths from user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code imports a helper module using a normal relative path. I did not find evidence of filesystem traversal or dynamic file access.

low_findings.title: Unicode Escape Is a Cursor Glyph
low_findings.description: The unicode escape finding is a default typewriter cursor character in a React component. It is not obfuscation or hidden executable content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The escape sequence is assigned to a visible cursor symbol prop. The surrounding code renders text animation state without decoding hidden payloads.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Markdown files include package manager commands such as npx, bunx, yarn, and pnpm. These are expected installation instructions and not automatic execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands appear only in fenced bash blocks. They are user-visible setup instructions for Remotion packages.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Fetch Patterns in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code fetches remote URLs and local static assets. This is a legitimate Remotion pattern, but copied code should restrict untrusted URLs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is semantically legitimate for media projects. The concern is contextual misuse in generated applications, not malicious skill behavior.

content.user_title: Build Remotion Videos with Best Practices
content.value_statement: Remotion projects often need precise media, timing, and layout patterns. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused rules for React video work.
actual_capabilities: Guides Remotion composition setup with props, metadata, dimensions, duration, and output names.
Explains importing images, videos, audio, GIFs, fonts, Lottie files, and static assets.
Covers timing patterns, sequencing, trimming, transitions, springs, interpolation, and frame-based animation.
Shows caption workflows for SRT parsing, display timing, word highlighting, and transcription options.
Provides examples for charts, Three.js scenes, text measurement, DOM measurement, and media metadata.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, but it does not render or export videos by itself.
Some examples require installing Remotion packages in the user project.
Remote media examples require network access and trusted source URLs.
The skill is not a replacement for testing renders in Remotion Studio or CI.

use_cases.title: Create a React Video Template
use_cases.description: Use the skill to structure compositions, props, assets, and timeline rules for a reusable Remotion video template.

use_cases.title: Improve Motion and Timing
use_cases.description: Apply interpolation, spring, transition, and sequence rules to make video animations deterministic and render-safe.

use_cases.title: Add Captions and Media Metadata
use_cases.description: Use caption, audio, video, and metadata patterns to build automated social video generation workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Composition
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Remotion best practices skill to design a basic composition with props, dimensions, duration, and static media assets.

prompt_templates.title: Animate a Scene
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Remotion scene and improve the timing, sequences, transitions, and frame-based animations using this skill.

prompt_templates.title: Add Captions and Audio
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a Remotion workflow that imports audio, parses captions, displays highlighted words, and keeps timing synchronized.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Video System
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this Remotion project for asset loading, metadata calculation, render determinism, media fetching, and production safety.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a product launch video with images, captions, and music.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes a Remotion composition plan with static assets, audio timing, caption pages, and frame-based transitions.

output_examples.input: A user has animations that flicker during rendering.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies non-deterministic animation sources and recommends driving motion from the current Remotion frame.

output_examples.input: A user needs duration based on a remote video.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests metadata calculation, trusted media URLs, abort handling, and fallback dimensions before rendering.

best_practices: Use frame-based animation with Remotion hooks so renders are deterministic.
Load and validate assets before rendering scenes that depend on media, fonts, captions, or Lottie data.
Keep composition props, metadata, dimensions, and duration explicit for predictable video exports.
anti_patterns: Do not use browser timers or self-running animation loops for rendered motion.
Do not fetch untrusted remote media without validation, timeouts, and fallback behavior.
Do not rely on CSS transitions or Tailwind animation classes for Remotion timeline animation.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants apply Remotion-specific rules for React video composition, assets, timing, captions, and media metadata.

faq.question: Does it generate finished videos?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance for Remotion projects. Rendering still happens in the user project or build pipeline.

faq.question: Can it help with captions?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers SRT import, caption display, word highlighting, and transcription options for Remotion workflows.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: The skill itself does not require network access. Some examples use remote media, documentation, or animation assets.

faq.question: Is it useful for Three.js in Remotion?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for React Three Fiber, ThreeCanvas, lighting, and frame-driven 3D animation.

faq.question: Which tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code because the skill is documentation-driven.
